title: Damper
subtitle: Australian bushman's bread, simple and satisfying
excerpt: Damper is a traditional Australian bread made with flour, water, and a little salt, originally cooked in the ashes of a campfire. The domestic version bakes in a hot oven in 25 minutes: a round, crusted loaf with a soft crumb. No yeast, no proving, no kneading beyond what brings the dough together.
sourceNotes: Damper is documented as a staple of Australian pastoral and bushman cooking from at least the 1820s. The name is believed to derive from the dampening effect of the unleavened dough on hunger during long journeys where no oven was available. Traditional recipes use nothing more than flour, water, and salt, baked in coals wrapped in wet hessian. Modern domestic recipes often add baking powder and milk or butter for a lighter, richer result. This recipe follows the traditional minimal formula with a small addition of baking powder.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Damper is best eaten the day it is made.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; bake at high heat for a good crust; done in 22 to 28 minutes

content.text: Damper is about as simple as bread gets: flour, water, salt, and heat. The baking powder lifts the loaf slightly and gives a lighter crumb than a purely unleavened round. The dough is soft, not particularly elastic, and should be brought together quickly without any real kneading. Bake at a high temperature for a thick, properly browned crust.

Heat the oven to 220 degrees C fan. Flour a baking tray. Combine 350 g self-raising flour, 1 tsp baking powder, and 1 tsp salt in a large bowl. If using butter, rub 30 g diced cold butter into the flour until the mixture resembles coarse crumbs. Make a well in the centre and pour in 200 ml cold water, adding it in two parts: pour in most of it and stir, then add more if the dough feels dry. Bring together into a rough ball with your hands, using only enough turns to make a cohesive dough. Turn out onto a floured surface and press into a round about 16 cm across and 5 cm deep.

Transfer to the floured tray. Score a shallow cross on top, no more than 1 cm deep. Bake for 22 to 28 minutes until the crust is deep gold and the base sounds hollow when tapped. Leave to rest for 5 minutes before tearing or cutting. Damper is best eaten warm, split and buttered or with golden syrup.

items.solution: The dough was overworked. Damper should be mixed until it just comes together. Treat it like scone dough: handle as little as possible.

items.problem: Crust is hard but the inside is still raw
items.solution: Score the cross more deeply, allowing heat to reach the centre. If this persists, reduce the oven to 200 degrees C after the first 15 minutes and bake for a further 10 to 15 minutes.
